text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Kate
Health Centre

Keele University, Keele, Newcastle

 Staffordshire ST5 5BG
Dear Dr Kate
Amelia Ford, DOB: 14-03-1988, Hospital No: K593274 NHS No. 7482109365
12 Maple Court, Wolstanton, Newcastle-under-Lyme, ST5 8RH 

Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset; comorbid anxiety/low mood as per patient report

MRI 2018: non-lesional, no focal cortical dysplasia or mesial temporal sclerosis identified
EEG 2019: intermittent left temporal sharp waves, increased with hyperventilation; no generalised spike-wave

Previous medication: carbamazepine (dermatological reaction), sodium valproate (weight gain, mood dulling), levetiracetam (irritability/anxiety), lamotrigine (partial benefit), clobazam PRN for clusters, lacosamide (dizziness), perampanel (behavioural change), topiramate (word-finding difficulty), zonisamide (appetite suppression)

Not taken: brivaracetam, oxcarbazepine, eslicarbazepine, cenobamate, pregabalin, tiagabine, vigabatrin, rufinamide

I reviewed Amelia Ford in clinic today, attending alone. She describes a fluctuating pattern of seizures over the past month with a notable deterioration in the last seven days, during which she experienced three tonic-clonic and one petit mal in last week. She reports these followed several nights of broken sleep and increased work-related stress. The focal seizures typically begin with a rising epigastric sensation, déjà vu, and brief speech arrest, occasionally progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. Postictal confusion lasts 30–60 minutes after the convulsions. There has been no recent head injury, infection, or medication non-adherence. She reports longstanding anxiety/low mood; she has not self-harmed and denies current suicidal ideation. She is open to psychological support and stresses that anxiety often precedes her focal auras.

Current medication: lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily; clobazam 10 mg at night PRN during high-risk periods (used twice in the last month); folic acid 5 mg once daily; omeprazole 20 mg once daily

Adverse effects: mild insomnia and occasional nausea with evening lamotrigine; no rash. She feels levetiracetam previously worsened anxiety, hence preference to avoid it and similar agents if possible.

Lifestyle/safety: good adherence to medication; no alcohol; sleeps 6 hours/night on average; no driving. Triggers noted: sleep deprivation, stress, and missed meals per patient diary. No pregnancies planned.

Examination today: alert and oriented; speech fluent; cranial nerves intact; normal tone, power and reflexes; gait normal. No clinical stigmata of systemic illness.

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily for now. We discussed options including brivaracetam or oxcarbazepine as next-line anti-seizure medicines given focal epilepsy. She wishes to consider these after reviewing written information.
- Short-term rescue: clobazam 10 mg at night for up to 3 nights around identifiable triggers; she already uses this judiciously.
- Mental health support: patient-reported anxiety/low mood is prominent and appears to modulate seizure threshold. I have signposted to local IAPT services and provided our epilepsy nurse contact for psychoeducation and self-management resources (sleep hygiene, structured routine, seizure diary). She is agreeable to a referral for talking therapy.
- Investigations: routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, vitamin D, TSH) to exclude reversible contributors to fatigue and mood; lamotrigine level not routinely required but can be considered if further dose adjustments occur.
- Safety advice reinforced: avoid sleep deprivation, maintain regular meals and hydration, and continue to refrain from driving. Seizure first-aid reviewed with her and she will share with family/colleagues.
- Follow-up: epilepsy nurse phone review in 4–6 weeks to track seizure frequency and tolerability; consultant review in 3 months or earlier if further deterioration or adverse effects. If seizures remain at the current rate (three tonic-clonic and one petit mal in last week pattern recurring), we will proceed to introduce an alternative focal epilepsy agent as above and consider repeat EEG.

Thank you for your help with ongoing care. Please let me know if there are any concerns in the interim.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: she experienced three tonic-clonic and one petit mal in last week
three tonic-clonic and one petit mal in last week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: 'she experienced three tonic-clonic and one petit mal in last week.' This provides a clear count over a one-week period. Applying the rule for handling multiple frequencies: these are different seizure types within the same time frame, so they should be summed when the frequencies are the same dimension (weekly). Therefore, total seizures in the last week = 3 + 1 = 4 per week. No other explicit higher-frequency period is provided. The plan section references the same weekly pattern as current rate, reinforcing this as the present frequency.
seizure_frequency_number: she experienced three tonic-clonic and one petit mal in last week
reference: three tonic-clonic and one petit mal in last week
